nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, clear to turbid slow-moving rivers, streams, and lake margins; often associated with submerged vegetation and marginal plant growth in highland plateau drainages

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Vallisneria, hornwort, water sprite)
floating plants for diffused light and refuge
open swimming areas for courtship display
driftwood or smooth rocks as cover

diet.notes: The Spotted Skiffia is an omnivore with a strong preference for plant-based foods. It accepts quality flake, micro-pellets, spirulina-based foods, and blanched vegetables such as zucchini, spinach, and lettuce. Supplementing with small live or frozen foods — daphnia, baby brine shrimp nauplii, or micro-worms — helps condition fish for breeding. In the wild, these fish graze on algae, detritus, small invertebrates, and plant matter. A varied diet with a heavy vegetable component supports long-term health.

goodWith: other Goodeids of similar size (Xenotoca eiseni, Zoogoneticus tequila)
small danios and rasboras tolerant of cooler water
avoidWith: large fast-moving fish that outcompete for food
tropical species requiring temperatures above 28°C (too warm for Skiffia)

imageCandidates.notes: Livebearerguy; 4272x2848. Tack-sharp adult male in a planted tank showing the diagnostic iridescent gold flank with bold black spotting/blotches - the hobbyist look; vibrant, whole fish in frame.
Livebearerguy; 1655x931. Crisp full-profile male against dark background, cleanly showing the gold-and-black spot pattern; slight crop but whole fish present.
Livebearerguy; 4272x2848. Male and olive-grey female together, illustrating the strong sexual dimorphism of this Goodeid; both fish sharp.

summary: The Spotted Skiffia is a Critically Endangered Mexican Goodeid livebearer from the Lerma-Chapala basin, now maintained almost entirely by conservation hobbyists. Males display gold-and-black spotting; females are olive-brown. Embryos develop internally via a placenta-like structure, making breeding moderately demanding.
careNotes:
The Spotted Skiffia (Skiffia multipunctata) is a small Goodeid livebearer endemic to the Lerma-Chapala basin of central Mexico, particularly Lago de Chapala and associated drainages. The species is considered Critically Endangered or Extinct in the Wild under IUCN criteria, due to catastrophic habitat loss driven by Lago de Chapala’s severe water-level declines, agricultural runoff, and introduced species. Captive populations maintained by conservation hobbyists represent the primary insurance against total extinction.

A minimum 60–75 L tank is generally recommended for a trio or small group, with a 60 cm footprint. This species needs cooler water than most tropical fish — 18–24°C is ideal, and sustained temperatures above 27°C stress the fish and reduce lifespan. Neutral to mildly alkaline, moderately hard water (pH 7.0–8.0, gH 10–25) reflects the species’ highland Mexican origins. Dense planting provides refuge and reduces inter-fish aggression; keeping 2–3 females per male eases pressure on individual females.

Because of the species’ conservation significance, hobbyists are strongly encouraged to register with coordinated breeding programs such as the Goodeid Working Group (GWG) and to source fish through GWG-registered keeper networks rather than the general trade. Avoid housing this species alongside other Goodeid livebearers where hybridisation is possible. Males display a distinctive spot pattern and iridescent gold-and-black colouration during courtship; females are olive-grey with subdued spotting. Males may occasionally nip at females outside the breeding period; the recommended 1:2 or 1:3 male-to-female ratio helps mitigate harassment.
breedingNotes: Like all Goodeids, the Spotted Skiffia is a true viviparous livebearer: embryos develop internally via trophotaeniae — ribbon-like fetal outgrowths that absorb uterine secretions from the mother, providing a placenta-like nutrient connection. Gestation lasts approximately 6–8 weeks at 22–24°C, with broods of 10–30 relatively large fry. Fry are born well-developed and free-swimming, capable of accepting micro-crushed flake, baby brine shrimp nauplii, and micro-worms from birth. No parental care is provided; females do not actively eat fry under well-fed conditions, but dense planting and a separate rearing container improve survival rates for conservation purposes. Conditioning pairs on a varied, protein-rich diet improves brood frequency. Males can exhibit persistent courtship pressure; a 1:2 male-to-female ratio is recommended. Females may produce 4–6 broods per year under good conditions. The interval between broods tends to be longer than in Poeciliid livebearers — the family that includes guppies and mollies — because the trophotaenial gestation is more resource-intensive for the female.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Skiffia multipunctata
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided water parameters (temperature 18–26°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness 100–250 mg/L), adult size (males to 6 cm, females to 7 cm), tank requirements, diet (omnivore, plant-heavy), breeding notes (trophotaeniae, gestation 6–8 weeks, 10–30 fry), conservation status (CR/EW), and native range (Lerma-Chapala basin, Mexico).

additional.name: FishBase — Skiffia multipunctata
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Goodeidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (to 7 cm TL), native range (Lerma-Chapala basin, Jalisco and Michoacan states, Mexico), IUCN status (Critically Endangered), temperature range (18–26°C), and diet classification. Confirmed species is listed as “Extinct in the Wild” in some regional assessments with captive populations in hobbyist studbooks.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Skiffia multipunctata
additional.notes: Conservation status assessment: Critically Endangered (CR) with some regional authorities listing as Extinct in the Wild (EW). Threats documented: Lago de Chapala water-level reduction, agricultural pollution, invasive species (tilapia, common carp). Notes relevance of captive conservation programs including European and Mexican zoos and the Goodeid Working Group hobbyist network.

additional.name: Goodeid Working Group (GWG) — Species Profile: Skiffia multipunctata
additional.notes: Specialist conservation hobbyist source. Confirmed studbook coordination, captive-population management guidelines, breeding recommendations (1:2 male-to-female ratio, cool water 20–24°C), and importance of genetic diversity maintenance across captive lines. Notes that this species is one of the GWG priority species for ex-situ conservation.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Goodeid Livebearer Care
additional.notes: General Goodeid care article referencing Skiffia multipunctata as an example conservation species. Confirmed tropical temperature avoidance (keep below 26°C), omnivore diet with vegetable emphasis, trophotaenial reproduction biology, and recommendation to source fish from GWG-registered keepers rather than commercial trade.

fish.breedingNotes: The Spotted Skiffia is a true viviparous Goodeid livebearer, with embryos nourished internally via trophotaeniae (ribbon-like fetal structures that provide a placenta-like nutrient connection). Gestation lasts approximately 6–8 weeks at 22–24°C, producing broods of 10–30 large, free-swimming fry that can accept micro-crushed flake and baby brine shrimp nauplii from birth. Maintaining a 1:2 male-to-female ratio is recommended to reduce courtship harassment. Conditioning breeders on a varied diet that includes protein-rich live or frozen foods improves brood frequency. Participating in Goodeid Working Group studbook programs is strongly encouraged for those keeping this species for conservation purposes.
